name: Ibn Battuta
primary_locations: Mecca and Medina (Hijaz; multiple Hajj performances)
Delhi Sultanate under Muhammad bin Tughluq
Mali Empire (Timbuktu, capital of Mansa Sulayman)
Fez (final residence under Abu Inan Faris)
institutions_founded_joined_opposed: Maliki madhhab (formed within it from youth; ancestral profession)
Network of Sufi zawiya/khanqah hospitality across Dar al-Islam (lodged, dressed as dervish)
Qadiship of Delhi under Muhammad bin Tughluq (appointed ~1334)
Qadiship of the Maldives (attempted enforcement of Maliki dress and prayer norms)
Marinid court of Abu Inan Faris, Fez (commissioned the Rihla, ~1355)
Diplomatic embassy from Delhi to Yuan China (1341, never fully delivered)

key_relationships.person: Muhammad bin Tughluq, Sultan of Delhi

key_relationships.relation_type: final patron; commissioned dictation of the Rihla
scribe/editor at Fez who gave the Rihla its literary form; died in 1348/1356 plague (sources vary)
Sufi sage whose prophecy of a brother in China haunted and propelled the journey
host-ruler whose parsimony Ibn Battuta criticized in the Rihla
brief near-contemporary encounter (c. 1350, Fez); intellectual foil — empiricist of the particular vs. theorist of the universal

key_relationships.person: Multiple wives and concubines across ten+ unions
key_relationships.relation_type: serial contracted marriages (Damascus, Delhi, Bukhara, Malabar, Maldives) + enslaved concubines, fathering children at many stops

intellectual_world: Fourteenth-century Marinid Maghreb on the western edge of a Dar al-Islam stitched together by Hajj routes, Sufi zawiyas, madrasa networks, and the hospitality protocols owed to a traveling scholar. Trained in Maliki fiqh — the living-practice-oriented school where 'amal ahl al-Madina (practice of the people of Medina) operates as a legal source — Ibn Battuta inhabits Ash'ari kalam (theology in which God's direct action can suspend secondary causation at any moment, making karamat of saints epistemically ordinary) and tasawwuf (Sufi discipleship, dervish dress, affiliation with shaykhs and their baraka). His conceptual world is post-Mongol Afro-Eurasia: the cosmopolitan Ilkhanid-Yuan corridor still open, the Black Death not yet arrived, the Delhi Sultanate at its terrifying apogee, and Mali's Mansa Sulayman receiving Maghribi jurists. Genre: rihla as established Arabic literary form (Ibn Jubayr as precedent); adab as the register of cultivated Islamic prose. Not yet available: nation, race in its modern scientific sense, secular political theory, individual rights, printed book, anthropology as discipline.
ontological_furniture: Allah's agency is immediate and continuous, not deist. Qadar (divine decree) is the real structure of what happens. The world is permeated by baraka (blessing-power) that inheres in sacred places (the Ka'ba, the Prophet's tomb, saints' shrines), in Qur'anic recitation, in the touch of a living wali (friend of God). Jinn are real. Dreams and prophecies (like Burhan al-Din's) are real epistemic channels. Karamat (miracles of saints) — levitating yogis, Sufi masters knowing distant events — are not 'superstition' to be explained away but phenomena consistent with Ash'ari occasionalism: God is the direct cause of every event, and whom He honors with karamat, He honors. The Dar al-Islam / Dar al-Harb distinction is not ideology but geography — the space where shari'a structures hospitality, marriage, food, and safe conduct vs. the space where it does not. The ummah is a real body, not a metaphor; a Maliki qadi from Tangier can arrive in Delhi or the Maldives and be fed, housed, married, and employed because he is a member of that body.

available_pathe.gloss: patient endurance under divinely-willed hardship; a virtue, not a mood
reliance on God; active-passive surrender of outcome after proper means are taken
divine testing; hardship read as God's examination of the servant (shipwreck, plague, tyrant's court)
the paired fear-of-God and hope-in-God that should both be present in the believer's heart
blessing-power; a real substance-like quality transferable through contact with saints, shrines, relics
divine decree; the metaphysical framing in which shipwreck, plague, and royal favor are all registered
cultivated Islamic comportment, propriety, literary refinement; the measure of whether a ruler or people are civilized
companionship; formal accompaniment of caravans, shaykhs, patrons — relational rather than psychological
modesty-shame before God and community; violations (e.g., Maldivian toplessness) register as jurisprudential, not prudish, distress
longing for the sacred (Mecca, the Prophet's tomb) and Sufi ecstatic finding; the affective register of dervish practice

framework_for_difficulty: Hardship is ibtilā' — God's testing of the servant — and the right response is ṣabr and tawakkul. When the ship sinks off the Malabar coast in 1347 and takes every diary, every gift from the Delhi sultan, and the Greek concubine pregnant with his child, this is qadar; the loss is not meaningless because nothing that God decrees is meaningless. The plague in Damascus in 1348 is registered as collective ibtilā', met with the city's 40,000-strong barefoot procession chanting the divine names — a response legible within a framework where disease is providential pedagogy, not epidemiology. Political terror (Muhammad bin Tughluq's capriciousness, the threat of execution) is survived through flight into Sufi retreat, fasting, and Qur'an recitation — not therapy, but ibtilā' answered with 'ibāda. Suffering has meaning because it is issued by the One who will judge.
model_of_selfhood: A juristically and genealogically constituted 'abd (servant, slave) of Allah, nested inside the ummah. Selfhood is not an introspective interior to be excavated but a standing defined by: (a) lineage and madhhab (Lawata Berber, Maliki, 'legal affairs are my ancestral profession'); (b) the body's ritual states (ṭahāra/purity, fasting, pilgrim ihrām); (c) position within webs of ṣuḥba, patronage, and scholarly ijaza; (d) nafs (the lower soul to be disciplined) versus rūḥ/qalb (spirit/heart to be polished). The 'I' of the Rihla is performative — qadi before sultans, dervish before crowds, bridegroom in five cities — and this multiplicity is not modern identity-plasticity but the normal functioning of a self constituted by its relations and roles under God. Vulnerability (terror at sea, loneliness, illness) is expressible but does not produce an 'inner life' in the post-Augustinian-cum-post-Romantic sense.
anachronisms_to_avoid: 'trauma' — the shipwreck is ibtilā' and qadar, reconstituted through scholarly brotherhood and the Burhan al-Din prophecy; pathologizing it imports a 20th-C van-der-Kolk schema alien to the voice
'career' — his movement is ṭalab al-'ilm / Hajj / service to the ummah, not an occupational trajectory with self-authored goals
'romance' — his ten+ unions are shar'i contracts and strategic kin-building; modern romantic-love vocabulary misreads them
'tolerance' / 'intolerance' — his evaluation of Greeks, Hindus, and heterodox Muslims runs on ḥalāl/ḥarām and bid'a, not on a liberal tolerance scale
'identity' — replace with madhhab + nasab + ṣuḥba + role; 'identity' imports a modern interiorized-ownership frame
'superstition' vs. 'science' — karamat of saints and yogic levitation are Ash'ari-consistent empirical reports, not failures of reason
'human rights' — his juristic universe is duty-and-hierarchy under shari'a; individual autonomy as ground of rights does not obtain
'self-discovery' as motive for travel — the overmastering impulse is framed as pilgrimage and 'ilm-seeking within a given cosmos, not as construction of a novel self
